title: [Quadlet] DefaultInstance is ignored when installing generated unit
body:
### Issue Description

I've defined a template unit `pcloud@.container` that I want to run multiple instances of. The unit has a `[Install]` section because I want a default instance based on the template to be enabled automatically. In order to specify a default instance, you can set [`DefaultInstance=`](https://www.freedesktop.org/software/systemd/man/systemd.unit.html#DefaultInstance=) in ordinary systemd units. This instance is effective when enabling the template unit without explicitly specifying an instance. 

Template units generated and automatically enabled by quadlet seem to ignore `DefaultInstance=`: The links created in e.g. `/run/systemd/generator/default.target.wants` are named like the plain template units (`pcloud@.service`), i.e. without setting the instance to the given default instance. Instead, I would wish that quadlet behaves consistently to `systemctl enable` in that it honors `DefaultInstance=` for template units, i.e. create a symlink named `pcloud@martin.service` if `DefaultInstance=martin` in the template unit.

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Create a template unit `foo@.container` including this section:
```
[Install]
WantedBy=default.target
DefaultInstance=example
```
2. `systemctl daemon-reload`


### Describe the results you received

`/run/systemd/generator/default.target.wants/foo@.service` is created.

### Describe the results you expected

`/run/systemd/generator/default.target.wants/foo@example.service` is created, being a symlink to `../foo@.service`.

### podman info output

comments.body:
Thanks for reaching out, @mfschumann!

I am not sure templates are supported by Quadlet. @alexlarsson PTAL
@vrothberg you are correct. Currently, Quadlet does not support unit templates.

How about adding a note in the man page about the missing support or issuing a warning when trying to run quadlet for template units then? Without that, people not knowing about the missing support (like me) may assume that everything worked as expected, but end up with an unexpected result (i.e. a unit like `foo@multi-user` appearing seemingly out of nowhere).

Btw, the documentation could make it clearer that adding an `[Install]` section in the `.container` file does not prepare the unit for being enabled (as for regular units), but directly enables it. That was quite unexpected for me, too.

> Btw, the documentation could make it clearer that adding an [Install] section in the .container file does not prepare the unit for being enabled (as for regular units), but directly enables it. That was quite unexpected for me, too.

Please see: https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html#enabling-unit-files. Let me know if you think a rephrase is needed.

> How about adding a note in the man page about the missing support or issuing a warning when trying to run quadlet for template units then? Without that, people not knowing about the missing support (like me) may assume that everything worked as expected, but end up with an unexpected result (i.e. a unit like foo@multi-user appearing seemingly out of nowhere).

I think we should decide if we would like to support templates. If not, then I think Quadlet should fail the processing of the file, not just issue a warning.

> I think we should decide if we would like to support templates. If not, then I think Quadlet should fail the processing of the file, not just issue a warning.

Interesting question.  Which attribute would be used to instantiate a template?  I have a hard time imaging atm.

> Which attribute would be used to instantiate a template? 

@mfschumann can you share some more information about what you were trying to achieve?

> > Btw, the documentation could make it clearer that adding an [Install] section in the .container file does not prepare the unit for being enabled (as for regular units), but directly enables it. That was quite unexpected for me, too.
> 
> Please see: https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-systemd.unit.5.html#enabling-unit-files. Let me know if you think a rephrase is needed.

This looks fine to me. I (or my podman 4.4.0 installation) must have missed the section when I had looked through the man page.

> > Which attribute would be used to instantiate a template?
> 
> @mfschumann can you share some more information about what you were trying to achieve?

I am using a cloud storage provider with multiple accounts and I want to back up the data stored there. 
Before using quadlet, I created a template unit for running the containerized cloud storage daemon and backup software using `podman generate systemd`. I could then use `systemd start` and `systemd enable` to instantiate units for the different accounts. I found that workflow quite comfortable.

I understand that because of systemd's design decision about enabling generated units this workflow is not achievable with quadlet. So when opening this issue I thought it would be a good idea to support the `DefaultInstance=` parameter, so that one instance is installed when running `systemctl daemon-reload`. Admittedly that is only a partial solution because it would create only one instance per template unit, which is probably not the most relevant use case (Why create a template unit if you only need one instance?). For enabling more instances you still have to resort to manually creating symlinks between `/etc/systemd/system` and `/run/systemd/generator` which is discouraged.

I think it would be more useful to introduce a new parameter in the `[Install]` section called `Instances=` which accepts a list of instances. When running `systemctl daemon-reload` quadlet will parse the parameter and instantiate the template unit by creating symlinks within `/run/systemd/generator`. That would avoid the discouraged dependency between static and generated config and provide a comfortable way to install instances of template units that is in line with the behavior of quadlet for ordinary, non-template units.

Would that be an anti-pattern by systemd?

@vrothberg @ygalblum WDYT?
@ygalblum Would `podman quadlet enable foo@ygal.container` create symlinks in `/etc/systemd/system` or in `/run/systemd/generator`?

I think it will follow what Quadlet does today. That is along side the location of the generated service file: `/run/systemd/generator` for system services and $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/generator for user services.

So, first it would have to receive a `-user` parameter to differentiate between them. In addition, since these paths are not guaranteed (according to the man page), it would have to be able to get an optional parameter for the location of the service files.

> I think it will follow what Quadlet does today. That is along side the location of the generated service file: `/run/systemd/generator` for system services and $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/generator for user services.

These locations are non-permanent, so quadlet has to recreate the symlinks after each reboot. That means it needs to keep track of what instances it has to create for each template unit. A straight-forward solution would be a list of instances somewhere in the `foo@.container` file. If you don't like adding quadlet-specific stuff to the `[Install]` section, maybe a new parameter in the `[Container]` section would do?
I think we should combine the discussions between #17662 and #17567. So, I opened a new discussion #17744
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@ygalblum Where are we on this one?  Are these issues still relevant?

> @ygalblum  Are these issues still relevant?

I would say so, but they are being tracked in #17744 now. So this issue can be closed.

We're waiting for a response from the systemd team. But, as @mfschumann wrote, I think this issue may be closed as it is being tracked in #17744. 

Hence, closing
